title: Making ‘Hobbs and Shaw’ the Action Movie Event of the Summer, With David Leitch | Interview
format: Sean Fennessey talks to director David Leitch about building Hobbs & Shaw as a comedy-first summer tentpole, coming up as a stuntman, the John Wick credit the DGA wouldn't give him, and why the Oscars still leave stunt work out.

segments.heading: David Leitch on stunts, credit, and comedy-forward action

films.title: Fast & Furious Presents: Hobbs & Shaw
films.note: The movie at hand. Leitch pitches it as a comedy-first summer spectacle built on the family-and-emotional-stakes earnestness of the Fast series. It used more stunt performers than anything he'd directed before: a 96-day shoot with roughly 1,600 VFX shots crammed into five months of post.

films.title: John Wick
films.note: Co-directed with Chad Stahelski for about $28M, sold on foreign pre-sales via Lionsgate International. The DGA let the pair co-direct and watched them do it on set, then denied Leitch a credit, calling the arrangement unprecedented. He's declined to fight it in court.

films.title: John Wick: Chapter 2
films.note: Stahelski stayed on the Wick train for the sequel; Leitch passed to chase Atomic Blonde, the point the two began working solo.

films.title: John Wick: Chapter 3 – Parabellum
films.note: Stahelski's; Leitch notes his partner had been on the show a few weeks earlier to discuss it.

films.title: Atomic Blonde
films.note: His first solo feature, from the graphic novel The Coldest City. He rewrote it toward 'neon noir' with a heavy music sensibility and calls it still the best filmmaking experience of his life.

films.title: Deadpool 2
films.note: The 'beloved franchise comedy' on his resume, proof he could do more than straight action. He was still in post on it when Hobbs & Shaw prep began.

films.title: Fight Club
films.note: His early break, doubling for Brad Pitt. Almost no stunt work in it (mostly photo-double bits), but he and the team trained Pitt and Edward Norton in basic fist-fighting for Fincher.

films.title: Ninja Assassin
films.note: Cited as the turning point for his second-unit career: a 35-to-40-day all-fights shoot handed to him and Stahelski by the Wachowskis, who they'd long mentored under.

films.title: Serenity
films.note: Joss Whedon's film, named as the pair's pioneering 'stunt-vis' job: shooting and cutting their own fight previews to show directors and studios.
One of the credits where their stunt-vis approach became their calling card before it was industry standard.
